description: Panic disorder is a serious anxiety condition marked by sudden; recurrent and unexpected panic attacks involving intense fear or discomfort that peaks within minutes. These attacks include physical symptoms such as rapid heartbeat; sweating; trembling; shortness of breath; chest pain; dizziness; and a sense of impending doom or loss of control; even without real danger. People often develop persistent worry about future attacks and may avoid situations where attacks previously occurred; leading to significant impairment in daily life; work; and relationships. The exact cause involves a combination of genetic factors; brain chemistry imbalances involving serotonin and norepinephrine; and environmental stressors or trauma. Early diagnosis and treatment with therapy and medication can greatly improve quality of life and reduce the frequency of attacks.

Vocal cord polyp is a benign; non-cancerous growth that forms on one or both vocal cords; typically due to voice overuse; irritation from acid reflux; smoking; or chronic coughing. It appears as a soft; swollen mass that disrupts normal vibration of the vocal folds; resulting in persistent hoarseness; breathy or raspy voice; vocal fatigue; and sometimes difficulty breathing if the polyp is large. Unlike nodules; polyps are usually unilateral and more vascular. They can interfere with singing; speaking; or daily communication and; in rare cases; cause airway obstruction. Diagnosis is confirmed by laryngoscopy; and while voice rest and speech therapy help; surgical removal is often required for larger or persistent polyps to restore normal voice quality.

Turner syndrome is a chromosomal disorder affecting females caused by the partial or complete absence of one X chromosome; leading to a wide range of developmental and medical issues. Key features include short stature; delayed or absent puberty due to ovarian failure; infertility; heart defects such as coarctation of the aorta; kidney abnormalities; hearing loss; and distinctive physical traits like a webbed neck; low-set ears; and widely spaced nipples. Additional problems can involve learning difficulties; especially in spatial and math skills; autoimmune conditions like thyroid disease; and osteoporosis risk from estrogen deficiency. It occurs randomly during conception and affects about 1 in 2;500 girls. Lifelong hormone replacement; growth hormone therapy; and regular cardiac and endocrine monitoring are essential for optimal health and development.

Cryptorchidism; also known as undescended testicle; is a common congenital condition in newborn boys where one or both testicles fail to descend from the abdomen into the scrotum before birth. It increases the risk of infertility; testicular cancer; and testicular torsion later in life if uncorrected. The condition may be unilateral or bilateral and is often linked to premature birth; low birth weight; or hormonal imbalances during fetal development. Most cases resolve spontaneously within the first few months; but persistent cases require surgical orchiopexy by age 1-2 years to preserve fertility and reduce cancer risk. Regular monitoring is important as the undescended testicle can affect normal testicular function and hormone production.

Poisoning due to ethylene glycol occurs when someone ingests this sweet-tasting chemical commonly found in antifreeze; leading to severe metabolic acidosis and multi-organ damage. Initial symptoms mimic alcohol intoxication including drunkenness; nausea; and vomiting; followed by rapid breathing; heart failure; kidney failure; seizures; coma; and potentially death within 24-72 hours if untreated. The toxic metabolites damage the kidneys; brain; and heart. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital care with fomepizole or ethanol to block metabolism; bicarbonate to correct acidosis; and hemodialysis to remove the toxin. Prevention focuses on safe storage of household chemicals away from children and pets.

Atrophic vaginitis; also called genitourinary syndrome of menopause; is a chronic condition caused by decreased estrogen levels after menopause or certain medical treatments; resulting in thinning; drying; and inflammation of the vaginal walls. Symptoms include vaginal dryness; itching; burning; painful intercourse (dyspareunia); urinary urgency; and recurrent urinary tract infections. The vaginal tissue becomes fragile and less elastic; increasing risk of tears and infections. It significantly affects quality of life and sexual health but is not an infection. Treatment typically involves topical estrogen therapy; vaginal moisturizers; or ospemifene to restore tissue health and relieve discomfort.

Fracture of the hand refers to a break in any of the small bones of the hand; including metacarpals or phalanges; usually resulting from trauma such as a fall; direct blow; or crush injury. Symptoms include severe pain; swelling; bruising; deformity; limited movement; and tenderness. Depending on the location and severity; it may affect grip strength and daily activities. Proper diagnosis via X-ray is essential; and treatment ranges from splinting or casting for stable fractures to surgical fixation with plates or screws for unstable or displaced breaks. Complications like stiffness; arthritis; or malunion can occur without timely intervention and rehabilitation.

Cellulitis or abscess of the mouth is a bacterial infection involving the soft tissues of the mouth; often originating from dental caries; trauma; or salivary gland blockage. It causes rapid swelling; severe pain; redness; warmth; pus formation; fever; and difficulty opening the mouth or swallowing. Untreated; the infection can spread to the neck or brain; leading to life-threatening complications like Ludwig's angina or sepsis. Common bacteria include streptococci and staphylococci. Immediate medical care with antibiotics; drainage of any abscess; and sometimes hospitalization is required to prevent airway obstruction and systemic spread.

Eye alignment disorder; medically known as strabismus; occurs when the eyes do not point in the same direction simultaneously; causing one eye to turn inward; outward; upward; or downward. It can be congenital or acquired due to muscle imbalance; nerve damage; or refractive errors. Symptoms include double vision; poor depth perception; headaches; and in children; amblyopia (lazy eye) if untreated. Long-term effects include impaired binocular vision and psychosocial issues from appearance. Treatment options include eyeglasses; patching; vision therapy; Botox injections; or surgery to realign the muscles for proper eye coordination.

Headache after lumbar puncture; known as post-lumbar puncture headache; is a common complication caused by leakage of cerebrospinal fluid through the puncture site in the lower back; leading to reduced intracranial pressure. It typically begins within 48 hours and is characterized by a severe; throbbing pain that worsens when sitting or standing and improves when lying flat. Associated symptoms may include nausea; vomiting; neck stiffness; and tinnitus. Most cases resolve spontaneously within a week with conservative measures like bed rest; hydration; and caffeine; but persistent cases may require an epidural blood patch to seal the leak.

Pyloric stenosis is a condition in infants where the pylorus muscle thickens; narrowing the outlet from the stomach to the small intestine and causing forceful; projectile vomiting after feeding. It usually develops between 2-8 weeks of age and leads to dehydration; weight loss; and electrolyte imbalances if not treated promptly. The exact cause is unknown but involves genetic and environmental factors. Diagnosis is confirmed by ultrasound showing the thickened pylorus. Treatment is surgical pyloromyotomy to relieve the obstruction; allowing normal feeding and growth with excellent long-term outcomes.

Salivary gland disorder encompasses conditions affecting the major salivary glands such as sialadenitis (inflammation); stones (sialolithiasis); tumors; or infections. Symptoms include painful swelling; dry mouth; difficulty swallowing; foul taste; and fever in acute cases. Causes range from bacterial infection; dehydration; and blockage by stones to autoimmune diseases like Sjogren's syndrome. Chronic cases can lead to gland damage and recurrent infections. Management involves antibiotics; hydration; sialagogues to promote saliva flow; stone removal; or surgery in severe cases to restore normal salivary function and prevent complications.

Osteochondrosis refers to a group of disorders involving disturbed blood supply to growing bone and cartilage; most commonly in children and adolescents; leading to necrosis and potential deformity. It affects areas like the spine (Scheuermann's disease); knee (Osgood-Schlatter); or hip (Legg-Calve-Perthes). Symptoms include localized pain; limping; swelling; and limited joint motion during growth spurts. The condition is self-limiting but can cause permanent joint damage if untreated. Treatment focuses on rest; physical therapy; bracing; and pain management to support natural healing and prevent long-term arthritis.

Injury to the knee includes sprains; strains; meniscus tears; ligament ruptures (ACL; MCL); or fractures from sports; falls; or accidents. It presents with pain; swelling; instability; locking; or popping sensations that impair walking and daily activities. Chronic cases can lead to osteoarthritis. Accurate diagnosis via physical exam; MRI; and X-ray guides treatment from conservative RICE (rest; ice; compression; elevation); physical therapy; and bracing to arthroscopic surgery for severe tears. Early intervention is critical to restore stability and prevent further joint degeneration.

Metabolic disorder is a broad category of conditions where the body's chemical processes for converting food to energy are disrupted; leading to abnormal levels of substances like sugars; fats; or amino acids. Examples include diabetes; phenylketonuria; and lysosomal storage diseases. Symptoms vary widely but may include fatigue; weight changes; developmental delays; organ damage; or recurrent infections. Genetic or acquired causes affect enzymes or hormones. Early screening and lifelong management with diet; medications; enzyme replacement; or gene therapy are essential to prevent complications such as organ failure or neurological impairment.

Vaginitis is inflammation of the vagina usually caused by infection (bacterial vaginosis; yeast; trichomonas); hormonal changes; or irritants. It leads to abnormal discharge; itching; burning; redness; swelling; and painful urination or intercourse. Untreated bacterial or parasitic types can ascend to cause pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility. Diagnosis involves pelvic exam and lab tests of discharge. Treatment is tailored—antibiotics; antifungals; or estrogen cream—along with avoiding irritants and practicing safe sex to restore normal vaginal flora and pH balance.

Sick sinus syndrome is a dysfunction of the heart's natural pacemaker (sinus node) causing abnormally slow or irregular heart rhythms (bradycardia; pauses; or alternating tachycardia). It primarily affects older adults and can result from aging; heart disease; medications; or infections. Symptoms include dizziness; fainting; fatigue; shortness of breath; and palpitations. Without treatment; it increases stroke and heart failure risk. Diagnosis uses ECG and Holter monitoring. Management often requires a permanent pacemaker implantation to regulate heartbeat and improve quality of life.

Tinnitus of unknown cause; or idiopathic tinnitus; is the perception of ringing; buzzing; or other noises in the ears without an external sound source. It may be constant or intermittent and is often linked to hearing loss; noise exposure; or vascular issues; though the exact mechanism remains unclear. It can cause significant distress; sleep disturbance; anxiety; and concentration problems. Evaluation rules out treatable causes like earwax or vascular tumors. Management includes sound therapy; cognitive behavioral therapy; hearing aids; and lifestyle changes to mask or habituate to the sound and reduce emotional impact.

Glaucoma is a group of eye diseases characterized by progressive damage to the optic nerve; usually due to elevated intraocular pressure; leading to irreversible vision loss if untreated. It is often called the 'silent thief of sight' because early stages are asymptomatic until peripheral vision is lost. Risk factors include age over 40; family history; diabetes; and high myopia. Types include open-angle (most common) and angle-closure (emergency). Treatment with eye drops; laser; or surgery aims to lower pressure and preserve remaining vision through lifelong monitoring.

Eating disorder refers to serious mental health conditions involving unhealthy relationships with food; weight; and body image; such as anorexia nervosa; bulimia; or binge-eating disorder. They cause severe physical complications including malnutrition; electrolyte imbalances; heart problems; and bone loss; alongside psychological distress. Genetic; psychological; and societal factors contribute. Symptoms include extreme restriction; purging; or compulsive overeating. Multidisciplinary treatment with nutritional counseling; psychotherapy (CBT); and medical monitoring is crucial for recovery and prevention of long-term organ damage or death.

Transient ischemic attack (TIA); often called a mini-stroke; is a temporary blockage of blood flow to the brain causing stroke-like symptoms such as sudden weakness; numbness; speech difficulty; or vision loss that resolve within minutes to hours. It serves as a critical warning sign for future full stroke. Caused by clots or narrowed arteries from atherosclerosis; hypertension; or atrial fibrillation. Immediate medical evaluation with imaging and blood tests is vital. Treatment focuses on antiplatelet drugs; blood pressure control; statins; and lifestyle changes to prevent permanent brain damage.

Pyelonephritis is a serious bacterial kidney infection; usually ascending from the bladder; causing high fever; chills; flank pain; nausea; vomiting; and painful urination. It can lead to sepsis; kidney scarring; or chronic kidney disease if recurrent or untreated. Common in women due to shorter urethra and often linked to urinary tract obstruction or stones. Diagnosis involves urine culture and imaging. Hospitalization with intravenous antibiotics is required for severe cases; followed by oral antibiotics and preventive measures like adequate hydration and prompt UTI treatment.

Rotator cuff injury involves damage to the group of muscles and tendons stabilizing the shoulder joint; caused by repetitive overhead motions; trauma; or age-related degeneration. Symptoms include shoulder pain; weakness; limited range of motion; and night pain. Partial or full-thickness tears can lead to chronic impingement or arthritis. Diagnosis uses MRI or ultrasound. Treatment starts with rest; physical therapy; anti-inflammatories; and corticosteroid injections; large or symptomatic tears often require arthroscopic repair to restore strength and prevent further degeneration.

Chronic pain disorder is persistent pain lasting over three months that significantly impacts physical; emotional; and social functioning; often without ongoing tissue damage. It may stem from conditions like fibromyalgia; neuropathy; or prior injury and involves central nervous system sensitization. Symptoms include constant aching; burning; or stabbing pain accompanied by fatigue; depression; and sleep disturbance. Multidisciplinary management with medications (antidepressants; anticonvulsants); physical therapy; cognitive behavioral therapy; and lifestyle modifications helps improve quality of life and reduce reliance on opioids.

Problem during pregnancy encompasses any complication threatening the mother or fetus; such as gestational diabetes; hypertension; preterm labor; or placental issues. Symptoms vary widely but may include abnormal bleeding; severe swelling; reduced fetal movement; or high blood pressure. Early detection through regular prenatal visits is critical to prevent miscarriage; stillbirth; or maternal morbidity. Management depends on the specific issue and may involve medications; bed rest; monitoring; or early delivery. Close obstetric care ensures the best outcomes for both mother and baby.

Liver cancer; primarily hepatocellular carcinoma; develops in the liver cells and is often linked to chronic hepatitis B/C; cirrhosis; alcohol abuse; or fatty liver disease. Early stages are asymptomatic; later symptoms include abdominal pain; weight loss; jaundice; fatigue; and swelling. It is aggressive with poor prognosis if advanced. Diagnosis involves imaging; blood tests (AFP); and biopsy. Treatment options include surgery; ablation; embolization; targeted therapy; immunotherapy; or liver transplant for eligible patients; emphasizing prevention through vaccination and lifestyle changes.

Atelectasis is the partial or complete collapse of a lung or lobe; preventing normal oxygen exchange; often due to blockage by mucus; tumor; or external pressure after surgery or prolonged bed rest. Symptoms include shortness of breath; rapid breathing; low oxygen levels; and chest pain. It increases risk of pneumonia. Diagnosis uses chest X-ray or CT. Treatment involves deep breathing exercises; incentive spirometry; chest physiotherapy; bronchoscopy to clear obstructions; or positive pressure ventilation to re-expand the lung tissue.

Injury to the hand refers to any trauma damaging bones; tendons; ligaments; nerves; or skin of the hand; commonly from cuts; fractures; or crush injuries. It leads to pain; swelling; bleeding; numbness; or loss of function affecting fine motor skills and grip. Prompt evaluation prevents infection; stiffness; or permanent disability. Treatment may include wound care; splinting; surgery for tendon repair; or antibiotics. Rehabilitation with occupational therapy is essential for restoring dexterity and preventing long-term complications like contractures.

Choledocholithiasis is the presence of gallstones in the common bile duct; causing obstruction that leads to severe right upper abdominal pain; jaundice; dark urine; pale stools; fever; and potential pancreatitis or cholangitis. It usually originates from gallbladder stones migrating into the duct. Diagnosis uses ultrasound; MRCP; or ERCP. Treatment involves endoscopic stone removal (ERCP with sphincterotomy); antibiotics if infected; and often cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence and complications like liver damage.

Injury to the hip includes fractures; dislocations; or soft tissue damage from falls; sports; or accidents; particularly common in older adults with osteoporosis. Symptoms are severe groin pain; inability to bear weight; swelling; and limited motion. Hip fractures require urgent surgical fixation or replacement to restore mobility and prevent complications like blood clots or pneumonia from immobility. Rehabilitation focuses on strengthening and balance to reduce future fall risk and maintain independence.

Cirrhosis is advanced scarring of the liver from long-term damage due to alcohol; hepatitis; or fatty liver disease; replacing healthy tissue with fibrous bands and nodules. It impairs liver function; causing fatigue; jaundice; ascites; easy bruising; confusion (encephalopathy); and increased infection risk. Portal hypertension leads to varices and bleeding. Diagnosis uses blood tests; imaging; and biopsy. Management includes treating the cause; medications for complications; nutrition; and liver transplant for end-stage disease to improve survival.

Thoracic aortic aneurysm is a localized bulging of the aorta in the chest that can rupture or dissect; often due to hypertension; atherosclerosis; connective tissue disorders; or bicuspid aortic valve. Many are asymptomatic until large; when they cause chest or back pain; hoarseness; or cough. Regular imaging monitors growth. Treatment involves blood pressure control with beta-blockers; lifestyle changes; and surgical repair or endovascular stent grafting when the aneurysm reaches a critical size to prevent life-threatening rupture.

Subdural hemorrhage is bleeding between the brain and its outer covering (dura); usually from head trauma tearing bridging veins; common in elderly or alcoholics due to brain atrophy. It can be acute (rapid deterioration) or chronic (gradual headache; confusion; weakness). Symptoms include headache; drowsiness; seizures; or hemiparesis. Diagnosis by CT scan. Treatment ranges from observation for small stable bleeds to surgical evacuation (burr hole or craniotomy) to relieve pressure and prevent brain herniation or permanent neurological deficits.

Diabetic retinopathy is damage to the retinal blood vessels from prolonged high blood sugar; leading to leakage; swelling; abnormal vessel growth; and eventual vision loss or blindness. Early non-proliferative stage may be asymptomatic; proliferative stage causes floaters; blurred vision; or dark spots. It is a leading cause of blindness in working-age adults. Annual eye exams are vital. Treatment includes laser photocoagulation; anti-VEGF injections; or vitrectomy surgery to preserve or restore vision while strict glycemic and blood pressure control prevents progression.

Fibromyalgia is a chronic disorder characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain; fatigue; sleep disturbances; memory issues; and mood problems without detectable inflammation or tissue damage. It involves abnormal central pain processing and is often triggered by stress; infection; or trauma. Tender points; stiffness; and co-existing conditions like IBS or migraines are common. Diagnosis is clinical after ruling out other causes. Multimodal treatment includes exercise; cognitive behavioral therapy; medications like duloxetine or pregabalin; and stress management to improve daily functioning.

Ischemia of the bowel; or mesenteric ischemia; occurs when blood flow to the intestines is reduced; causing sudden severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam findings; bloody diarrhea; and vomiting. Acute cases from embolism or thrombosis are surgical emergencies; chronic from atherosclerosis leads to post-meal pain and weight loss. Untreated; it progresses to bowel necrosis; perforation; and sepsis. Diagnosis uses CT angiography. Treatment includes fluids; anticoagulants; angioplasty; or resection of dead bowel to restore circulation and save life.

Fetal alcohol syndrome is a permanent birth defect resulting from maternal alcohol consumption during pregnancy; causing growth deficiency; distinctive facial features (smooth philtrum; thin upper lip; small eyes); central nervous system abnormalities; and behavioral problems. Affected children have intellectual disability; learning difficulties; poor coordination; and increased risk of ADHD or mental health issues. No amount of alcohol is safe. Prevention through abstinence is key; early intervention with therapy; education; and support services helps maximize potential and manage lifelong challenges.

Peritonitis is inflammation of the peritoneum; the lining of the abdominal cavity; usually from bacterial infection after perforation of the intestine; appendix; or other organ. It causes sudden severe abdominal pain; fever; rigid abdomen; nausea; and shock. It is a life-threatening emergency requiring hospitalization. Diagnosis uses physical exam; blood tests; and imaging. Treatment involves broad-spectrum antibiotics; intravenous fluids; and urgent surgery to repair the source and wash out the abdomen to prevent sepsis and multi-organ failure.

Injury to the abdomen can be blunt (from car accidents or falls) or penetrating (stab or gunshot wounds); damaging organs like liver; spleen; intestines; or blood vessels. Symptoms include abdominal pain; bruising; distension; vomiting blood; or shock. Internal bleeding or perforation can lead to peritonitis or hemorrhagic shock. Rapid assessment with FAST ultrasound or CT is essential. Management ranges from observation and supportive care to emergency laparotomy for repair or removal of damaged organs to stabilize the patient and prevent death.

Acute pancreatitis is sudden inflammation of the pancreas; most often caused by gallstones or heavy alcohol use; leading to severe upper abdominal pain radiating to the back; nausea; vomiting; and fever. Enzymes autodigest the pancreas; causing swelling and potential systemic complications like organ failure. Diagnosis uses blood tests (elevated amylase/lipase) and imaging. Treatment is supportive with NPO status; IV fluids; pain control; and nutritional support; severe cases may need ICU care or procedures to remove gallstones or necrotic tissue.

Thrombophlebitis is inflammation of a vein with clot formation; often superficial in the legs after injury or IV use; or deep (DVT). It causes pain; redness; swelling; and a palpable cord-like vein. Superficial cases are usually self-limited but can extend to deep veins. Deep vein involvement risks pulmonary embolism. Diagnosis by ultrasound. Treatment includes compression; anti-inflammatories; anticoagulants for DVT; and elevation to reduce swelling and prevent complications like chronic venous insufficiency.

Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease causing recurrent episodes of wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; and coughing; often triggered by allergens; exercise; cold air; or infections. It involves reversible bronchoconstriction and mucus production. Uncontrolled asthma can lead to severe attacks requiring hospitalization. Diagnosis uses spirometry and peak flow monitoring. Long-term control with inhaled corticosteroids; long-acting bronchodilators; and quick-relief albuterol; plus trigger avoidance and action plans; allows most patients to lead normal active lives.

Foreign body in the vagina is the presence of an object (toy; tampon; tissue) causing irritation; foul-smelling discharge; bleeding; pain; or infection. In children it may be accidental; in adults often retained tampon or contraceptive device. Prolonged retention can lead to toxic shock syndrome or fistula. Diagnosis by pelvic exam. Treatment is gentle removal; antibiotics if infected; and education on hygiene to prevent recurrence and complications like scarring.

Restless leg syndrome (RLS) is a neurological disorder causing an irresistible urge to move the legs; accompanied by uncomfortable sensations like crawling or tingling; worse at rest or evening; and relieved by movement. It disrupts sleep and quality of life; often linked to iron deficiency; pregnancy; or kidney disease. Diagnosis is clinical. Treatment includes lifestyle changes; iron supplementation if deficient; and medications like dopamine agonists (pramipexole) or gabapentin to reduce symptoms and improve sleep.

Emphysema is a progressive lung disease; part of COPD; where destruction of alveolar walls leads to enlarged air spaces; reduced gas exchange; and trapped air. Primarily caused by long-term cigarette smoking or alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. Symptoms include severe shortness of breath; chronic cough; barrel chest; and fatigue. It is irreversible but manageable. Treatment focuses on smoking cessation; bronchodilators; pulmonary rehabilitation; oxygen therapy; and in advanced cases lung volume reduction surgery or transplant to improve breathing and slow decline.

Cysticercosis is a parasitic infection caused by the larval stage of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium; acquired by ingesting eggs in contaminated food or water. Larvae form cysts in muscles; brain (neurocysticercosis); or eyes; leading to seizures; headaches; hydrocephalus; or vision loss. It is a leading cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic areas. Diagnosis uses imaging and serology. Treatment with antiparasitic drugs (albendazole); steroids to reduce inflammation; and antiepileptics controls symptoms and prevents complications like stroke or blindness.

Induced abortion is the deliberate termination of a pregnancy before the fetus can survive outside the uterus; performed for medical; personal; or social reasons using medications (mifepristone + misoprostol) or surgical procedures (aspiration or D&E). It is safe when done by trained providers under legal conditions; with low complication rates. Post-procedure care includes monitoring for bleeding or infection and contraception counseling. Emotional support may be needed. Legal and access issues vary globally; emphasizing the importance of safe; regulated services to protect women's health.

Teething syndrome occurs in infants as primary teeth erupt through the gums; causing irritability; excessive drooling; gum swelling; mild fever; and sleep disturbance. It does not cause high fever or severe illness; other causes should be ruled out. Symptoms typically begin around 6 months and continue until age 2-3. Management includes gentle gum massage; chilled teething rings; and over-the-counter pain relief like acetaminophen or ibuprofen. Good oral hygiene from the first tooth prevents future dental issues.

Infectious gastroenteritis; commonly called stomach flu; is inflammation of the stomach and intestines due to viruses (norovirus; rotavirus); bacteria; or parasites from contaminated food or water. It causes watery diarrhea; vomiting; abdominal cramps; fever; and dehydration. Most cases are self-limited but can be severe in infants; elderly; or immunocompromised. Treatment focuses on oral rehydration solutions; rest; and probiotics; antibiotics only for specific bacterial causes. Prevention through hand hygiene; safe food practices; and vaccination (rotavirus) reduces spread.

Acute sinusitis is inflammation of the sinus cavities lasting up to 4 weeks; usually following a viral upper respiratory infection; causing facial pain/pressure; nasal congestion; thick discharge; reduced smell; cough; and fever. Bacterial superinfection occurs in some cases. Diagnosis is clinical or with imaging if complications suspected. Most resolve with supportive care (saline irrigation; decongestants; pain relief); antibiotics are reserved for severe or persistent bacterial cases to avoid resistance. Untreated; it can lead to orbital or intracranial spread.

Substance-related mental disorder includes conditions like intoxication; withdrawal; or substance-induced psychosis; depression; or anxiety triggered by alcohol; opioids; stimulants; or other drugs. Symptoms range from altered consciousness and hallucinations to mood swings and cognitive impairment. Chronic use leads to dependence and long-term brain changes. Diagnosis follows DSM criteria after ruling out primary psychiatric illness. Treatment involves detoxification; counseling; medications for withdrawal (e.g.; benzodiazepines for alcohol); and long-term rehabilitation to achieve and maintain sobriety.

Postpartum depression is a serious mood disorder occurring within the first year after childbirth; characterized by persistent sadness; anxiety; irritability; sleep/appetite changes; guilt; and in severe cases thoughts of harming self or baby. Hormonal shifts; sleep deprivation; and psychosocial factors contribute. It affects up to 15% of mothers and can impair bonding and infant development. Screening is routine. Treatment with antidepressants (SSRIs safe in breastfeeding); psychotherapy; and support groups promotes recovery and family well-being.

Coronary atherosclerosis is the buildup of plaque in the coronary arteries supplying the heart; narrowing them and reducing blood flow; leading to angina; heart attack; or sudden death. Risk factors include smoking; hypertension; high cholesterol; diabetes; and family history. Symptoms may be absent until advanced. Diagnosis uses stress testing; angiography; or CT calcium scoring. Management includes lifestyle changes; statins; antiplatelets; beta-blockers; and procedures like stenting or bypass surgery to prevent myocardial infarction and improve survival.

Spondylitis; particularly ankylosing spondylitis; is a chronic inflammatory arthritis primarily affecting the spine and sacroiliac joints; causing progressive stiffness; pain; and potential fusion of vertebrae. It often starts in young adults with morning stiffness improving with activity. Extra-articular features include uveitis and enthesitis. Genetic factor HLA-B27 is strongly associated. Early diagnosis with MRI and blood tests allows treatment with NSAIDs; biologics (TNF inhibitors); exercise; and physical therapy to maintain posture and mobility.

Pituitary adenoma is a usually benign tumor of the pituitary gland that can be hormone-secreting (prolactinoma; growth hormone) or non-functioning; causing hormonal imbalances or mass effects like headaches and vision loss from optic chiasm compression. Symptoms depend on the type—galactorrhea; acromegaly; Cushing's; or hypopituitarism. Diagnosis uses MRI and hormone assays. Treatment options include medications (cabergoline for prolactinomas); transsphenoidal surgery; or radiation to normalize hormones and preserve vision.

Uterine fibroids are non-cancerous growths in the uterus composed of smooth muscle and connective tissue; common in reproductive-age women. They can cause heavy menstrual bleeding; pelvic pain; pressure; infertility; or pregnancy complications depending on size and location. Many are asymptomatic. Diagnosis by ultrasound or MRI. Management ranges from watchful waiting and medications (tranexamic acid; GnRH agonists) to minimally invasive procedures like embolization or myomectomy; or hysterectomy for severe cases.

Idiopathic nonmenstrual bleeding refers to unexplained vaginal bleeding outside of normal menses; often due to hormonal imbalance; polyps; or endometrial issues without identifiable cause after evaluation. It can be spotting or heavy and may signal underlying problems like cancer in older women. Thorough workup with ultrasound; biopsy; or hysteroscopy is required. Treatment depends on findings but may include hormonal therapy or D&C to control bleeding and rule out malignancy.

Chalazion is a painless; chronic lump in the eyelid caused by blockage and inflammation of a meibomian oil gland; leading to a localized swelling that may distort vision if large. Unlike stye; it is not infected. Warm compresses and massage often resolve small ones; persistent cases need steroid injection or surgical drainage. Good eyelid hygiene prevents recurrence. It is benign but can be confused with other eyelid tumors requiring evaluation.

Ovarian torsion is the twisting of the ovary on its ligamentous supports; cutting off blood supply and causing sudden severe pelvic pain; nausea; and vomiting. It is a surgical emergency; most common in women with ovarian cysts or during pregnancy. Delayed diagnosis can lead to ovarian necrosis and loss. Ultrasound with Doppler helps confirm reduced blood flow. Laparoscopic detorsion and cystectomy; or oophorectomy if non-viable; restores blood flow and preserves fertility when possible.

diseases: retinopathy due to high blood pressure
description: Retinopathy due to high blood pressure (hypertensive retinopathy) damages retinal blood vessels from chronic or severe hypertension; causing arteriolar narrowing; hemorrhages; exudates; and optic disc swelling. It is a marker of systemic vascular damage and can lead to vision loss. Early stages are asymptomatic; advanced causes blurred vision. Control of blood pressure with medications is the primary treatment; along with regular eye exams to monitor and prevent progression to irreversible damage.

Vaginal yeast infection; usually caused by Candida albicans; results from overgrowth of yeast due to antibiotics; diabetes; pregnancy; or immunosuppression. Symptoms include thick white discharge; intense itching; redness; and soreness. It is not sexually transmitted but can be uncomfortable. Diagnosis by microscopic exam or culture. Treatment with antifungal creams; suppositories; or oral fluconazole provides rapid relief; probiotics and avoiding irritants help prevent recurrence.

Mastoiditis is a serious bacterial infection of the mastoid bone behind the ear; usually complicating untreated acute otitis media. It causes severe ear pain; swelling behind the ear; fever; headache; and possible drainage. Untreated; it can lead to hearing loss; meningitis; or brain abscess. Diagnosis uses CT scan. Treatment requires intravenous antibiotics and often surgical mastoidectomy to drain pus and remove infected bone; preventing life-threatening intracranial spread.

Lung contusion is bruising of the lung tissue from blunt chest trauma; causing bleeding into alveoli and impaired gas exchange. Symptoms include shortness of breath; chest pain; cough with bloody sputum; and low oxygen levels. It can worsen over hours and lead to pneumonia or ARDS. Diagnosis by chest X-ray or CT. Management is supportive with oxygen; pain control; fluid restriction; and mechanical ventilation if severe; most resolve in 7-10 days with careful monitoring to avoid complications.

Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) is a genetic heart muscle disease where the septum thickens; obstructing left ventricular outflow and causing arrhythmias. Symptoms include chest pain; shortness of breath; fainting; and sudden cardiac death risk; especially during exercise. Diagnosis uses echocardiography. Treatment with beta-blockers or verapamil reduces obstruction; severe cases need surgical myectomy; alcohol septal ablation; or ICD implantation for prevention of fatal arrhythmias.

Ingrown toe nail occurs when the nail edge grows into the surrounding skin; causing pain; redness; swelling; and possible infection; commonly on the big toe from improper trimming or tight shoes. Chronic cases lead to granulation tissue. Treatment starts with soaking; proper nail care; and cotton wedge placement; severe or infected cases require partial nail removal under local anesthesia. Preventive footwear and trimming techniques reduce recurrence.

Pulmonary eosinophilia involves infiltration of eosinophils into the lungs; often due to parasitic infections; drugs; or idiopathic causes like eosinophilic pneumonia. Symptoms include cough; shortness of breath; fever; and wheezing. Chest X-ray shows infiltrates. Diagnosis includes blood eosinophil count and bronchoalveolar lavage. Treatment with corticosteroids and antiparasitics (if applicable) resolves symptoms; identifying and removing triggers prevents relapse.

Corneal disorder encompasses conditions affecting the clear front layer of the eye; such as infections; dystrophies; abrasions; or keratoconus; leading to pain; redness; blurred vision; light sensitivity; or scarring. The cornea is vital for focusing light. Diagnosis uses slit-lamp exam. Treatment varies from antibiotic drops for infection to corneal transplant for advanced scarring; aiming to restore clarity and prevent vision loss.

diseases: foreign body in the gastrointestinal tract
description: Foreign body in the gastrointestinal tract refers to swallowed objects (coins; batteries; bones) that may lodge in the esophagus; stomach; or intestines; causing obstruction; perforation; or bleeding. Symptoms depend on location—drooling; pain; vomiting; or abdominal tenderness. Most pass spontaneously; sharp or battery objects require urgent endoscopic removal to prevent serious complications like fistula or peritonitis. Prevention in children involves supervision and safe toy storage.

Endophthalmitis is a severe intraocular infection; often post-surgical or from trauma; involving the vitreous and retina; causing rapid vision loss; pain; redness; and floaters. Bacterial or fungal. It is an ophthalmic emergency. Diagnosis by clinical exam and vitreous tap. Treatment requires intravitreal antibiotics/antifungals and often vitrectomy to clear infection and preserve vision; delay can result in blindness.

Intestinal malabsorption is impaired absorption of nutrients in the small intestine due to celiac disease; Crohn's; pancreatic insufficiency; or infections; leading to diarrhea; weight loss; bloating; fatigue; and deficiencies (vitamins; iron). Diagnosis involves blood tests; endoscopy; and biopsy. Treatment targets the cause (gluten-free diet for celiac; enzymes for pancreatic issues) plus nutritional supplements to correct deficiencies and restore normal weight and energy.

Viral warts are benign skin growths caused by human papillomavirus (HPV); appearing as rough; raised lesions on hands; feet (plantar); or face. They are contagious via skin contact and can spread or persist for years. Treatment options include salicylic acid; cryotherapy; imiquimod cream; or laser; some resolve spontaneously with immune response. Prevention through HPV vaccination reduces certain types.

Hyperhidrosis is excessive sweating beyond normal thermoregulation; affecting palms; soles; underarms; or face; causing social embarrassment; skin maceration; and infections. It can be primary (idiopathic) or secondary to medical conditions. Diagnosis is clinical. Treatment includes antiperspirants; iontophoresis; oral medications (glycopyrrolate); Botox injections; or endoscopic thoracic sympathectomy for severe refractory cases to improve quality of life.

Stroke is a medical emergency where blood flow to part of the brain is interrupted by clot (ischemic) or rupture (hemorrhagic); causing sudden numbness; weakness; confusion; vision loss; or severe headache. Rapid treatment with clot-busters or thrombectomy within hours can minimize brain damage. Risk factors include hypertension; smoking; and atrial fibrillation. Rehabilitation and secondary prevention with antiplatelets and lifestyle changes are critical for recovery and reducing recurrence.

Pilonidal cyst is a chronic abscess or sinus in the natal cleft near the tailbone; often containing hair; causing pain; swelling; drainage; and recurrent infection; especially in young men. It results from hair penetration into skin. Treatment includes incision and drainage for acute abscess; or surgical excision for chronic cases to prevent recurrence. Good hygiene and hair removal help reduce risk.

Crushing injury occurs when a body part is compressed by heavy force; causing muscle damage; bleeding; and compartment syndrome. It can lead to rhabdomyolysis; kidney failure from myoglobin release; and shock. Immediate care involves fluid resuscitation; pain control; and fasciotomy if needed. Long-term complications include contractures and nerve damage requiring physical therapy.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus is a condition in older adults with enlarged brain ventricles and normal CSF pressure; causing the classic triad of gait disturbance; urinary incontinence; and dementia. It is potentially reversible with ventriculoperitoneal shunting. Diagnosis uses MRI; lumbar puncture trial; and gait analysis. Early intervention improves mobility and cognition; untreated leads to progressive disability.

Alopecia refers to hair loss from any cause; including autoimmune (areata); androgenetic (male/female pattern); or scarring types. It can be patchy or diffuse; affecting scalp or body. Psychological impact is significant. Diagnosis by exam and biopsy if needed. Treatments include minoxidil; corticosteroids; JAK inhibitors; or hair transplant depending on type to promote regrowth or camouflage.

Hashimoto thyroiditis is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the thyroid gland; leading to hypothyroidism with symptoms of fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; constipation; and dry skin. It is the most common cause of hypothyroidism and more prevalent in women. Diagnosis by elevated TSH and anti-TPO antibodies. Lifelong levothyroxine replacement normalizes hormone levels and prevents complications like goiter or heart issues.

Flat feet (pes planus) is a condition where the arches of the feet collapse; causing the entire sole to touch the ground. It can be congenital or acquired from injury or obesity; leading to foot pain; fatigue; and ankle problems. Many are asymptomatic. Treatment includes supportive shoes; orthotics; stretching; and rarely surgery to reconstruct the arch and relieve pain.

Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is the inflammatory form of fatty liver disease not caused by alcohol; progressing from simple steatosis to fibrosis and cirrhosis. Linked to obesity; diabetes; and metabolic syndrome. Often asymptomatic until advanced; then fatigue; right upper pain; or jaundice. Diagnosis by biopsy or non-invasive tests. Management emphasizes weight loss; exercise; and medications like vitamin E or pioglitazone; advanced cases may need transplant.

Hemarthrosis is bleeding into a joint space; often from trauma; hemophilia; or anticoagulation; causing sudden swelling; pain; warmth; and limited motion. Repeated episodes lead to joint destruction and arthritis. Diagnosis by aspiration or imaging. Treatment includes rest; ice; factor replacement in bleeding disorders; and aspiration to relieve pressure and prevent chronic damage.

Pelvic organ prolapse occurs when pelvic floor muscles weaken; allowing the bladder; uterus; or rectum to descend into the vagina; causing bulging; pressure; urinary incontinence; or bowel issues; common after childbirth or menopause. Diagnosis by pelvic exam. Treatment ranges from pelvic floor exercises and pessary to surgical repair to restore support and improve symptoms.

Fracture of the arm involves break in the humerus; radius; or ulna from falls or trauma; presenting with pain; deformity; swelling; and inability to move. Open fractures risk infection. Diagnosis by X-ray. Treatment includes closed reduction and casting or surgical fixation with plates/rods; followed by physical therapy to regain strength and prevent stiffness.

Coagulation (bleeding) disorder includes conditions like hemophilia or von Willebrand disease where clotting factors are deficient; leading to prolonged bleeding after injury; easy bruising; or spontaneous joint/muscle bleeds. Diagnosis by coagulation tests. Management with factor concentrates; desmopressin; or antifibrinolytics prevents life-threatening hemorrhage and joint damage.

Intracranial hemorrhage is bleeding within the skull; including subdural; epidural; or intracerebral; often from trauma; hypertension; or aneurysm rupture. It causes sudden severe headache; neurological deficits; seizures; or coma. Diagnosis by urgent CT. Treatment involves blood pressure control; reversal of anticoagulants; and neurosurgical evacuation to reduce pressure and prevent herniation.

Hyperkalemia is elevated blood potassium level; often from kidney failure; medications; or tissue breakdown; causing muscle weakness; arrhythmias; or cardiac arrest. Diagnosis by serum potassium and ECG changes. Urgent treatment with calcium gluconate for heart protection; insulin-glucose to shift potassium; and kayexalate or dialysis for removal prevents fatal complications.

Cornea infection (keratitis) is inflammation usually from bacteria; viruses; or fungi after trauma or contact lens overuse; causing pain; redness; tearing; blurred vision; and light sensitivity. It can lead to scarring and vision loss. Diagnosis by slit-lamp and culture. Treatment with fortified antibiotic drops or antivirals; plus cycloplegics; is aggressive to preserve corneal clarity.

Abscess of the lung is a pus-filled cavity from bacterial infection; often after pneumonia or aspiration; causing fever; productive cough with foul sputum; chest pain; and weight loss. Diagnosis by chest CT. Treatment requires prolonged antibiotics and drainage (percutaneous or bronchoscopic) to resolve infection and prevent complications like empyema.

Dengue fever is a mosquito-borne viral illness causing high fever; severe headache; eye pain; joint/muscle pain; and rash. Severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever) involves plasma leakage; bleeding; and shock. No specific antiviral; supportive care with fluids and acetaminophen (avoid aspirin) is key. Prevention focuses on mosquito control and vaccination in endemic areas.

Chronic sinusitis is inflammation of the sinuses lasting over 12 weeks; causing nasal congestion; facial pressure; reduced smell; and post-nasal drip. It may involve polyps or fungal elements. Diagnosis by endoscopy or CT. Treatment includes nasal corticosteroids; saline irrigation; antibiotics for bacterial flares; and endoscopic surgery for refractory cases to improve drainage and quality of life.

Cholesteatoma is a skin growth in the middle ear from chronic infection or eustachian tube dysfunction; eroding bone and causing hearing loss; dizziness; and facial nerve damage if untreated. Diagnosis by otoscopy. Surgical removal is required to prevent intracranial complications like meningitis.

Volvulus is twisting of the intestine (usually sigmoid or midgut in infants) causing obstruction and potential ischemia. Symptoms include sudden severe pain; vomiting; and distension. Diagnosis by X-ray or CT. Emergency surgery to untwist or resect dead bowel is lifesaving; delay leads to perforation and peritonitis.

Injury to the finger includes fractures; dislocations; tendon lacerations; or nail bed damage from crushing or cuts; leading to pain; deformity; and loss of function. Prompt X-ray and specialist care prevent malunion or stiffness. Treatment may involve splinting; surgery; or therapy for full recovery.

Atrial fibrillation is an irregular; often rapid heart rhythm originating in the atria; increasing stroke risk from clot formation. Symptoms include palpitations; fatigue; and shortness of breath. Diagnosis by ECG. Treatment with rate control (beta-blockers); rhythm control; and anticoagulation (DOACs) reduces complications and improves quality of life.

Pinworm infection (enterobiasis) is a common intestinal parasitic infection causing intense perianal itching; especially at night; from female worms laying eggs. Highly contagious in households. Diagnosis by tape test. Treatment with mebendazole or albendazole for the patient and family; plus hygiene measures like handwashing and laundering bedding; eradicates the infection.

Urethral valves are congenital folds in the male urethra obstructing urine flow; causing bladder distension; hydronephrosis; and kidney damage if untreated. Diagnosis prenatally or by voiding cystourethrogram. Endoscopic ablation relieves obstruction and preserves renal function.

Open wound of the neck from trauma risks major vessel or airway injury; causing bleeding; swelling; or breathing difficulty. Immediate control of hemorrhage and surgical exploration if deep is required to prevent exsanguination or infection.

Achalasia is a motility disorder where the esophagus fails to relax and propel food; causing dysphagia; regurgitation; chest pain; and weight loss. Diagnosis by barium swallow and manometry. Treatment with pneumatic dilation; Heller myotomy; or Botox provides symptom relief by improving esophageal emptying.

Conductive hearing loss occurs when sound waves are not properly conducted through the outer or middle ear due to wax; infection; otosclerosis; or trauma; leading to reduced hearing volume. Diagnosis by audiometry. Treatment with cerumen removal; antibiotics; or surgery restores hearing in most cases.

Abdominal hernia is protrusion of intestine or fat through a weak spot in the abdominal wall (inguinal; umbilical; incisional); causing a bulge; pain; or obstruction. Surgical repair prevents incarceration or strangulation.

Cerebral palsy is a group of permanent movement disorders from non-progressive brain damage before or during birth; causing spasticity; weakness; or ataxia. Multidisciplinary therapy; medications; and surgery improve mobility and independence.
Marijuana abuse involves problematic use leading to dependence; cognitive impairment; or exacerbation of mental health issues. Counseling and support programs aid cessation.

Cryptococcosis is a fungal infection from Cryptococcus; primarily affecting lungs or brain in immunocompromised patients; causing meningitis-like symptoms. Antifungal therapy (amphotericin then fluconazole) is prolonged.
Obesity is excessive body fat (BMI ≥30) increasing risks of diabetes; heart disease; and joint problems. Lifestyle; medications (semaglutide); or bariatric surgery promote sustainable weight loss.
Indigestion (dyspepsia) causes upper abdominal discomfort; bloating; or nausea from overeating; GERD; or ulcers. Antacids; PPIs; and dietary changes relieve symptoms.
Bursitis is inflammation of a bursa sac from overuse or trauma; causing localized pain and swelling. Rest; ice; NSAIDs; and aspiration provide relief.
Esophageal cancer often presents late with dysphagia and weight loss; treatment combines chemotherapy; radiation; and surgery.
Pulmonary congestion from heart failure causes fluid backup in lungs; leading to shortness of breath. Diuretics and heart failure therapy improve breathing.
Juvenile rheumatoid arthritis causes joint inflammation in children; managed with NSAIDs; methotrexate; and biologics to prevent deformity.
Actinic keratosis are precancerous skin lesions from sun exposure; cryotherapy or topical 5-FU prevents progression to squamous cell carcinoma.
Acute otitis media is middle ear infection causing ear pain and fever; antibiotics are used judiciously while most resolve with supportive care.
Astigmatism is irregular corneal curvature causing blurred vision at all distances; corrective lenses or surgery improve focus.
Tuberous sclerosis is a genetic disorder causing benign tumors in brain; skin; and organs; mTOR inhibitors and antiepileptics control seizures.
Empyema is pus in the pleural space from pneumonia; drainage and antibiotics are required.
Presbyacusis is age-related sensorineural hearing loss; hearing aids improve communication.
Neonatal jaundice from elevated bilirubin is common and treated with phototherapy to prevent kernicterus.
COPD from smoking causes irreversible airflow limitation; bronchodilators; steroids; and oxygen therapy manage symptoms.
Dislocation of the elbow requires prompt reduction and immobilization to restore function.
Spondylosis is degenerative spine changes causing neck or back pain; conservative therapy with PT and medications is first-line.
Herpangina is a viral illness with mouth ulcers and fever in children; supportive care suffices.
Injury to the shoulder ranges from rotator cuff tears to dislocations; imaging guides physical therapy or surgery.
Poisoning due to antidepressants requires supportive care and specific antidotes like sodium bicarbonate for TCA overdose.
Infection of open wound presents with pus and redness; antibiotics and debridement prevent spread.
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a clot in a deep vein; anticoagulants prevent pulmonary embolism.
Protein deficiency causes edema; muscle wasting; and impaired immunity; dietary supplementation corrects it.
Myoclonus is sudden muscle jerks; antiepileptics or clonazepam control symptoms.
Bone spur of the calcaneous (heel spur) causes heel pain; orthotics and stretching relieve pressure.
Von Willebrand disease impairs platelet function and clotting; desmopressin or factor replacement manages bleeding.
Open wound of the back requires cleaning; antibiotics if infected; and monitoring for spinal involvement.
Heart block disrupts electrical conduction; pacemaker is often needed for symptomatic cases.
Colonic polyp is a growth in the colon; removal during colonoscopy prevents cancer.
Magnesium deficiency causes muscle cramps and arrhythmias; oral or IV replacement restores levels.
Female infertility of unknown cause is evaluated with ovulation induction using clomiphene or letrozole.
Pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardium causing chest pain; NSAIDs and colchicine treat it.
Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) impairs focus and impulse control; stimulants or behavioral therapy help.
Pulmonic valve disease may require valve replacement if severe.
Tietze syndrome causes costochondral swelling and pain; NSAIDs and local injection relieve symptoms.
Cranial nerve palsy causes facial weakness or double vision; steroids or treatment of underlying cause is used.
Injury to the arm is managed with immobilization and rehabilitation.
Conversion disorder presents with neurological symptoms without organic cause; psychotherapy is primary treatment.
Complex regional pain syndrome involves chronic pain after injury; gabapentinoids and physical therapy manage it.
Otosclerosis causes conductive hearing loss; stapedectomy improves hearing.
Injury to the trunk requires evaluation for internal organ damage.
Hypothyroidism from thyroid underactivity is treated with levothyroxine.
Primary insomnia is difficulty sleeping without medical cause; CBT-I and short-term hypnotics help.
Lice infestation is treated with permethrin or oral ivermectin plus environmental cleaning.
Vitamin B12 deficiency causes anemia and neuropathy; injections or oral supplements correct it.
Diabetes involves high blood sugar; metformin; insulin; and lifestyle changes control it.
Vulvodynia is chronic vulvar pain; topical lidocaine or antidepressants provide relief.
Endometriosis causes pelvic pain and infertility; hormonal therapy or surgery reduces lesions.
Vasculitis is inflammation of blood vessels; immunosuppressants control it.
Concussion is mild traumatic brain injury; rest and gradual return to activity are recommended.
Oral leukoplakia is a white patch that may be precancerous; biopsy and removal are advised.
Chronic kidney disease progresses to failure; ACE inhibitors and diet slow decline.
Bladder disorder includes overactive bladder; anticholinergics like oxybutynin treat urgency.
Chorioretinitis is retinal inflammation; often from toxoplasmosis; antimicrobials and steroids are used.
Priapism is prolonged erection; aspiration or medications detumescence the penis.
Myositis is muscle inflammation; steroids and immunosuppressants treat it.
Mononucleosis from EBV causes fatigue and sore throat; supportive care is sufficient.
Neuralgia is nerve pain; gabapentin or carbamazepine relieves it.
Polycystic kidney disease causes cysts and hypertension; tolvaptan slows progression.
Bipolar disorder involves mood swings; mood stabilizers like lithium manage episodes.
Amyloidosis involves protein deposits; chemotherapy or supportive care depending on type.
Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) causes weakness; IVIG or steroids treat it.
Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) causes heartburn; PPIs and lifestyle changes control it.
Vitreous hemorrhage causes sudden vision loss; underlying cause (diabetes) is treated; often with vitrectomy.
Poisoning due to antimicrobial drugs requires supportive care and discontinuation.
Open wound of the mouth heals with oral hygiene and antibiotics if infected.
Scleroderma causes skin thickening and organ involvement; immunosuppressants manage symptoms.
Myasthenia gravis causes muscle weakness; pyridostigmine and immunosuppressants improve strength.
Hypoglycemia is low blood sugar; glucose or glucagon reverses it.
Idiopathic absence of menstruation (amenorrhea) is evaluated with hormonal therapy.
Dislocation of the ankle requires reduction and stabilization.
Carbon monoxide poisoning causes headache and confusion; 100% oxygen is the antidote.
Panic attack is sudden intense fear; benzodiazepines or breathing techniques abort it.
Plantar fasciitis causes heel pain; stretching; orthotics; and night splints relieve it.
Hyperopia (farsightedness) is corrected with glasses.
Poisoning due to sedatives may require flumazenil for benzodiazepines.
Pemphigus is autoimmune blistering; steroids and rituximab control it.
Peyronie disease causes penile curvature; collagenase injections or surgery straighten it.
Hiatal hernia causes reflux; PPIs or surgery if severe.
Extrapyramidal effect of drugs (dystonia) is treated with benztropine.
Meniere disease causes vertigo and hearing loss; diuretics and low-salt diet help.
Anal fissure causes painful defecation; nitroglycerin ointment heals it.
Allergy triggers histamine release; antihistamines and avoidance prevent symptoms.
Chronic otitis media involves persistent ear infection; tympanostomy tubes may be needed.
Fracture of the finger is immobilized or surgically fixed.
Hirschsprung disease causes constipation from missing nerve cells; surgery removes affected bowel.
Polymyalgia rheumatica causes shoulder/hip stiffness; low-dose prednisone is effective.
Lymphedema causes swelling; compression and manual drainage manage it.
Bladder cancer is treated with BCG immunotherapy or chemotherapy.
Acute bronchospasm is relieved by albuterol and steroids.
Acute glaucoma is an emergency treated with eye drops and laser.
Open wound of the chest requires sterile dressing and monitoring for pneumothorax.
Dislocation of the patella is reduced and braced.
Sciatica is nerve pain from spinal compression; NSAIDs and PT help.
Hypercalcemia is treated with hydration and bisphosphonates.
Stress incontinence is managed with pelvic exercises or duloxetine.
Varicose veins cause leg discomfort; compression or ablation treats them.
Benign kidney cyst is monitored or aspirated if symptomatic.
Hydrocele of the testicle is fluid collection; surgery drains it if large.
Degenerative disc disease causes back pain; conservative therapy is first-line.
Hirsutism is excess hair; oral contraceptives or spironolactone reduce it.
Dislocation of the foot requires reduction.
Hydronephrosis from obstruction is relieved by stent or surgery.
Diverticulosis is managed with high-fiber diet.
Pain after an operation is controlled with multimodal analgesia.
Huntington disease is genetic; tetrabenazine reduces chorea.
Lymphoma is treated with chemotherapy regimens like R-CHOP.
Dermatitis due to sun exposure is treated with topical steroids.

diseases: anemia due to chronic kidney disease
description: Anemia due to chronic kidney disease is corrected with erythropoietin.
Injury to internal organ requires imaging and possible surgery.
Scleritis is eye inflammation; steroids treat it.
Pterygium is conjunctival growth; surgery removes it if vision affected.
Fungal infection of the skin is treated with topical antifungals.
Insulin overdose causes hypoglycemia; glucose is given.

diseases: syndrome of inappropriate secretion of adh (siadh)
description: Syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (SIADH) causes hyponatremia; fluid restriction or tolvaptan corrects it.
Foreign body in the ear is removed carefully.
Premenstrual tension syndrome is managed with SSRIs or OCPs.
Orbital cellulitis is an emergency requiring IV antibiotics.
Injury to the leg is splinted and rehabilitated.
Hepatic encephalopathy is treated with lactulose.
Bone cancer requires chemotherapy and surgery.
Syringomyelia involves spinal cord cyst; surgery drains it.
Pulmonary fibrosis causes scarring; antifibrotics like nintedanib slow progression.
Mitral valve disease may need valve repair or replacement.
Parkinson disease causes tremor and rigidity; levodopa is mainstay.
Gout causes acute joint pain; colchicine and allopurinol prevent attacks.
Otitis media is middle ear infection; antibiotics if bacterial.
Drug abuse (opioids) is treated with buprenorphine or methadone.
Myelodysplastic syndrome causes anemia; supportive transfusions or azacitidine.
Fracture of the shoulder is managed with sling or surgery.
Acute kidney injury requires fluids and cause-specific treatment.
Threatened pregnancy is monitored with progesterone support if indicated.
Intracranial abscess needs surgical drainage and antibiotics.
Gum disease (periodontitis) is treated with scaling and antibiotics.
Open wound from surgical incision is monitored for infection.
Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is managed with PPI and endoscopy.
Seborrheic dermatitis is treated with antifungal shampoo.
Drug abuse (methamphetamine) requires supportive counseling.
Torticollis causes neck spasm; Botox injections help.
Poisoning due to antihypertensives is treated supportively.
Tension headache is relieved by NSAIDs and stress reduction.
Alcohol intoxication requires supportive care and thiamine.
Scurvy from vitamin C deficiency causes bleeding gums; ascorbic acid cures it.
Narcolepsy causes daytime sleepiness; modafinil treats it.
Food allergy is managed with avoidance and epinephrine.
Labyrinthitis causes vertigo; meclizine provides symptomatic relief.
Anxiety is treated with SSRIs and CBT.
Impulse control disorder benefits from SSRIs.
Stenosis of the tear duct causes tearing; surgery opens it.
Abscess of nose requires drainage.
Omphalitis in newborns needs antibiotics.
Leukemia is treated with chemotherapy.
Bell palsy causes facial paralysis; prednisone and antivirals speed recovery.
Conjunctivitis due to allergy is treated with antihistamine drops.
Drug reaction may require antihistamines or steroids.
Adrenal cancer is managed with surgery and mitotane.
Myopia (nearsightedness) is corrected with lenses.
Osteoarthritis causes joint pain; acetaminophen and exercise help.
Thyroid disease is managed with hormone replacement or antithyroid drugs.
Pharyngitis is often viral; antibiotics only if strep.
Chronic rheumatic fever requires penicillin prophylaxis.
Hypocalcemia is corrected with calcium and vitamin D.
Macular degeneration causes central vision loss; anti-VEGF injections treat wet type.
Pneumonia is lung infection; antibiotics target the organism.
Cold sore from HSV is treated with acyclovir.
Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) are managed with beta-blockers if symptomatic.
Testicular cancer is highly curable with surgery and chemotherapy.
Hydrocephalus requires shunt placement.
Breast cancer is treated with surgery; chemo; and hormonal therapy.
Anemia due to malignancy is managed with erythropoietin or transfusion.
Esophageal varices are treated with banding and beta-blockers.
Endometrial cancer is managed with hysterectomy and chemo.
Cystic fibrosis requires enzymes; antibiotics; and CFTR modulators.
Intertrigo (skin condition) is treated with antifungals and barrier creams.
Parathyroid adenoma causes hypercalcemia; surgery removes it.
Glucocorticoid deficiency requires hydrocortisone replacement.
Temporomandibular joint disorder causes jaw pain; NSAIDs and splints help.
Wilson disease causes copper accumulation; chelators like penicillamine treat it.
Vesicoureteral reflux is managed with prophylactic antibiotics or surgery.
Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness; supplements correct it.
Gonorrhea is treated with ceftriaxone plus azithromycin.
Fracture of the rib is managed with pain control and breathing exercises.
Ependymoma is a brain tumor treated with surgery and radiation.
Hepatitis due to a toxin (e.g.; acetaminophen) is treated with N-acetylcysteine.
Vaginal cyst is excised if symptomatic.
Open wound of the shoulder is cleaned and sutured.
Ectopic pregnancy is treated with methotrexate or surgery.
Chronic knee pain benefits from physical therapy and analgesics.
Pinguecula is a conjunctival growth; artificial tears suffice unless inflamed.
Hypergammaglobulinemia is managed by treating underlying cause.
Alcohol abuse is treated with counseling and medications like naltrexone.
Carpal tunnel syndrome causes hand numbness; splinting or surgery relieves pressure.
Pituitary disorder requires hormone replacement as needed.
Kidney stone passes with tamsulosin and hydration or is removed.
Autism is managed with behavioral therapy; risperidone for irritability.
Cat scratch disease is treated with azithromycin if severe.
Chronic glaucoma is controlled with eye drops to lower pressure.
Retinal detachment requires urgent surgery.
Aplastic anemia is treated with immunosuppression or stem cell transplant.
Overflow incontinence is managed with catheterization or alpha-blockers.
Hemolytic anemia is treated with steroids or splenectomy.
Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) is managed with rest and steroid injection.
Open wound of the eye requires specialist repair.
Syphilis is cured with penicillin.
Diabetic kidney disease is slowed by ACE inhibitors and SGLT2 inhibitors.
Nose disorder (rhinitis) is treated with nasal steroids.
Drug withdrawal is managed symptomatically (e.g.; clonidine for opioids).
Dental caries is treated with fillings.
Hypercholesterolemia is treated with statins.
Fracture of the patella may require surgical fixation.
Kidney failure requires dialysis or transplant.
Fracture of the neck is immobilized urgently.
Muscle spasm is relieved by cyclobenzaprine.
Hemophilia requires factor replacement.
Hyperosmotic hyperketotic state (HHS) is treated with fluids and insulin.
Peritonsillar abscess requires drainage and antibiotics.
Gastroparesis is managed with metoclopramide and small meals.
Itching of unknown cause is treated with antihistamines.
Polycythemia vera is managed with hydroxyurea and phlebotomy.
Thrombocytopenia is treated with steroids or IVIG.
Head and neck cancer is treated with chemoradiation.
Pseudohypoparathyroidism requires calcium and vitamin D.
Goiter is managed with levothyroxine or surgery.
Urge incontinence is treated with anticholinergics.
Edward syndrome is a chromosomal disorder with supportive care only.
Open wound of the arm is sutured and dressed.
Muscular dystrophy is managed with steroids and supportive therapy.
Mittelschmerz (ovulation pain) is relieved by NSAIDs.
Corneal abrasion heals with antibiotic ointment.
Anemia of chronic disease is treated with erythropoietin.
Dysthymic disorder is managed with antidepressants.
Scarlet fever is treated with penicillin.
Hypertensive heart disease requires blood pressure control.
Drug abuse (barbiturates) needs taper and supportive care.
Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) is managed with OCPs and metformin.
Encephalitis is treated with antivirals or supportive care.
Cyst of the eyelid is incised if needed.
Balanitis is treated with antifungals or steroids.
Foreign body in the throat requires endoscopic removal.
Drug abuse (cocaine) is managed supportively.
Optic neuritis is treated with steroids.
Alcohol withdrawal is managed with benzodiazepines.
Premature atrial contractions (PACs) are usually benign.
Hemiplegia requires stroke rehabilitation.
Hammer toe is corrected with surgery if severe.
Open wound of the cheek is sutured carefully.
Joint effusion is aspirated if large.
Open wound of the knee needs thorough cleaning.
Meningioma is often observed or surgically removed.
Brain cancer is treated with surgery; radiation; and chemo.
Placental abruption requires urgent delivery.
Seasonal allergies (hay fever) are treated with antihistamines.
Lung cancer is managed with targeted therapy or surgery.
Primary kidney disease is treated according to biopsy findings.
Uterine cancer is treated with hysterectomy.
Dry eye of unknown cause is treated with artificial tears.
Fibrocystic breast disease is managed with NSAIDs.
Fungal infection of the hair (tinea capitis) is treated with oral antifungals.
Tooth abscess requires drainage and antibiotics.

diseases: envenomation from spider or animal bite
description: Envenomation from spider or animal bite needs antivenom and supportive care.
VACTERL syndrome requires surgical correction of defects.
Vertebrobasilar insufficiency is managed with antiplatelets.
Rectal disorder is evaluated by proctoscopy.
Atonic bladder is treated with bethanechol or catheterization.
Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is treated with Epley maneuver.
Blepharospasm is managed with Botox.
Sarcoidosis is treated with steroids if symptomatic.
Metastatic cancer is managed with palliative chemotherapy.
Trigger finger (finger disorder) is treated with steroid injection.
Stye is treated with warm compresses.
Hemochromatosis is managed with phlebotomy.
Osteochondroma is observed or excised if symptomatic.
Cushing syndrome is treated by removing the cause.
Typhoid fever is treated with antibiotics.
Vitreous degeneration is observed.
Atrophic skin condition is managed with moisturizers.
Aspergillosis is treated with voriconazole.
Uterine atony is treated with oxytocin.
Trichinosis is treated with albendazole.
Whooping cough is treated with macrolides.
Open wound of the lip is sutured.
Subacute thyroiditis is self-limited and treated with NSAIDs.
Oral mucosal lesion requires biopsy if persistent.
Open wound due to trauma is cleaned and closed.
Intracerebral hemorrhage is managed with blood pressure control.
Alzheimer disease is treated with donepezil.
Vaginismus is managed with pelvic floor therapy.
Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is treated with hydroxychloroquine.
Premature ovarian failure requires hormone replacement.
Thoracic outlet syndrome is managed with PT or surgery.
Ganglion cyst is aspirated or excised.
Dislocation of the knee requires reduction.
Crohn disease is treated with biologics and steroids.
Postoperative infection is treated with antibiotics.
Folate deficiency is corrected with folic acid.
Fluid overload is treated with diuretics.
Atrial flutter is managed like atrial fibrillation.
Skin disorder is treated according to diagnosis.
Floaters are observed unless sudden increase.
Tooth disorder requires dental evaluation.
Heart attack is treated with reperfusion therapy.
Open wound of the abdomen may need surgical exploration.
Fracture of the leg is casted or fixed.
Oral thrush (yeast infection) is treated with nystatin.
Pityriasis rosea is self-limited.
Allergy to animals is managed with avoidance and antihistamines.
Orthostatic hypotension is treated with midodrine.
Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is treated with CPAP.
Hypokalemia is corrected with potassium supplements.
Psoriasis is managed with topical steroids or biologics.
Dislocation of the shoulder is reduced.
Intussusception is reduced by air enema or surgery.
Cervicitis is treated with antibiotics if STI.
Abscess of the pharynx requires drainage.
Primary thrombocythemia is managed with hydroxyurea.
Arthritis of the hip is treated with NSAIDs or replacement.
Decubitus ulcer requires wound care and pressure relief.
Hypernatremia is corrected slowly with fluids.
Sensorineural hearing loss is managed with hearing aids.
Chronic ulcer is treated with compression and wound care.
Osteoporosis is prevented with bisphosphonates and calcium.
Ileus is managed supportively.
Sickle cell crisis is treated with hydration and pain control.
